Okay, so here's the deal. When I first started working at that penny stock firm many years ago, calling people who had no money, basically moms and pops with little or no net worth, right? Then about eight months later, when I opened up my own firm and I found that murky middle ground, which was selling $5 stocks to the richest 1% of Americans, then boom just like that, the thing took off like a rocket. Well, basically it was an untapped market niche I found and by teaching my brokers the straight line system, it was so effective that it almost became ridiculous. It fed on itself and it fed on itself, it kept feeding on itself and before I knew it, I had almost a thousand people working for me, all in one massive room and all of them were using the straight line at the highest possible level and closing basically anyone who was closeable. And the whole thing went just absolutely wild. Now that's the short story. In real life though, it didn't quite happen that instantly. It was a bit of a lag in the beginning when I was training my guys using my old methods and they were still great at the time, but they weren't that magic bullet I needed to take my guys that really weren't that sharp and turn them into world-class closers. But still every morning, every evening, I would still give them these hour-long sales meetings on how to close rich people and once a week I'd give them one of these marathon sessions at night where I'd stand in front of the room and for three or four hours I'd drill the hell out of them, right? But no matter what I did, no matter how much training they got, they just still couldn't seem to close rich people. They were young and educated and they were basically getting batted around and abused by the rich people. I think they were actually getting hung up on more than they were, but anyway it was really driving me crazy because I had this huge problem that I could close these guys and so could my junior partner, Danny, my one superstar salesman. He was a boring closer like me. He struggled a bit in the beginning, but once he got over the hump he was unstoppable. It was like both he and I would just nuke instinctively what to say to people and exactly when to say it and also how to say it. We were born close as the phrase goes, but what does it really mean when you say, you know, he's a boring closer or she's a boring closer? Is it a God-given gift preserved for only the select lucky few or does everyone have the gift? Everyone has the potential to be a boring closer. It's just a matter of figuring out how to wake up the potential inside you. Thankfully it turns out to be the latter and not the former or else I'd be out of a job and I'll tell you why in a second, but first let me explain to you exactly what it means to be a boring closer. It means that without even realizing it yourself, your brain has somehow figured out a strategy that can move people from a state of being not so certain about something to a state of being absolutely certain about something. I'll repeat that again. Born close is using unconscious strategy, meaning inside them, to move people from a state of being not so certain about something to a state of being absolutely certain about something. And that's why it makes total sense that I was able to break all those sales records back in the day. You see, despite the fact that I hadn't even invented the straight line yet, it didn't matter whether I was knocking on doors, selling penny stocks over the phone or closing the richest 1% of Americans on buying millions of dollars of six dollar stocks. I was still actually using a straight line strategy on them. I was just doing it unconsciously up here. I didn't realize it at the time and the same thing went for Danny. Once I showed him a few things, his brain connected the rest of the dots and he began using straight line tactics without even knowing it. The problem with that though is that if you're doing something really, really well without even thinking about it, it's nearly impossible to teach it to another person. That's what some of the greatest sales people in the world are the worst sales trainers. They're expert closers themselves, but if you ask them how do you do it, how do you close many people, they'll just throw their hands up in the air and say, I don't know, I'm just a really good people person or I'm just passionate about my product or I'm really good at making people feel comfortable or getting into a poor or one of the other hundred or so sales cliches. But the truth is they just don't really know. They're all running unconscious strategies so they can't pass that knowledge, that system or skill set on to someone else. So the fact that Danny and I can go out and sell rich people didn't really help me that much. I mean, yeah, it was good that we made some sales, made some money, but my vision was to train thousands of people to sell to rich people. I wanted to duplicate myself and the only way to do that was to take what I was doing so well unconsciously and turn it into what's called a conscious strategy. So anyway, going back to teaching my guys at Stratton, after a month of trying to teach them how to call rich people, they were still failing miserably. And again, it wasn't due to a lack of training. I was giving two meetings a day plus every other week I'd give them one of these four hour marathon sessions at night where I'd stand in front of the room and I'd teach them how to close. And the lesson there is that when it comes to sales training, it's not just about quantity, it's also about quality. And these were the days right before I hit on the straight line system and I tried everything, everything I could think of, but there was this missing ingredients, this X factor and I just couldn't quite hit on it. Then one day at a sheer frustration, I remember this day like it was yesterday, my guys had asked me to do one of these emergency night training sessions, which is basically the marathon at night. They were all getting really negative from not being able to close rich people, which is still by the way one of the biggest issues I deal with today when I go into a sales force doing consulting. I walk into a company and have an amazingly bright future, great product, great everything, right? Perfect industry, perfect timing. And yet the sales force is still negative because they just don't have the tools, they don't actually close the people. And to those people, just so you know, the straight lines like mana from heaven. It's like pouring water on a wilted flower. Anyway, my sales will bugging me like craze at night and I knew myself that I was about to end up back in the poor house, so to speak, if I couldn't teach my guys how to close rich people. So I was like, all right, all right, fine guys, I'll do what I'll do it. And you know, let's go home, we'll change, we'll get this street closed for us. We came back to work at about 7pm, we were gonna work till 11. And I stood in front of the room and I started doing my usual training, which I had to do mostly with teaching them how to sound good, the right tonalities and such and also answering objections, which they'd been getting a ton of from the rich people since they started calling them, right? So I was doing my typical role playing back and forth and saying, all right, guys, if someone says I want to think about them, what do you say? And one of the guys would spit back some canned response like, well, Mr. Jones, I understand you want to think about it, but time is a critical factor here and blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, you know, one of those sales cliches. And I'm like, no, that's wrong, your totality is off, you get a break report them. And I would then I would demonstrate the exact right response, how to say it and then move on to the next guy with a different objection and on and on it went. And I could see from their faces, they were so frustrated, they just simply weren't not getting it and they just couldn't close. And finally, I was like, you know, guys, you're making this so much harder than it actually is. It's not that complicated, which a few of the guys started mumbling how it is so hard and the rich people are all idiots and assholes and, you know, how we should go back to calling poor people will kinder and gentler and happy to send what a few bucks they have to us to a complete stranger on the mere phone call, right? And they kept on whining at me, something just clicked inside me. And that was it. Bam. That was the trigger. I mean, the thought of abandoning my billion dollar idea and going back to calling poor people. It got me so angry inside that I popped into a state of such absolute certainty that I was nearly screaming at them. I was like, what are they all saying to it's making this so damn impossible to close them? Go ahead, tell me, tell me, tell me, they just looked at me with these kind of fearful expressions on their faces. I seem like I was about to literally take a flying leap at them and start choking to death, right? But after a few seconds, one of them muttered, well, there are so many objections, they just keep throwing them at us. To which another guy chimed in, yeah, they just keep throwing these objections at us. Another one said, yeah, exactly. It's all these thousands of objections. You know, we never know what to expect, what they're going to say. It's like we're just getting objections rained down on us, right? We're getting barraged. And I'm like, really? I'm like, it's what a content was dripping from my tone. Tell me, what are they going? Tell me what these thousands of objections are. Come on, tell me, tell me. And they began giving me all the common objections that all salesmen hear. I want to think about it. I want to call you back. I got to speak to my accountant, my lawyer, I got to talk to my wife, bedtime a year right now, tax time, Christmas time, on and on their way, listing all thousand of these objections, which at the end of the day, turned out to be actually only 12. That's right. There were only 12 objections. And the rest of them were just all repeats or slight variations of those basic 12 objections. So I shook my head and absolute disgust. And I said, you guys are just too much. You're crying. I always objection. There are only 12 of them. And even those don't matter. I mean, don't you guys get it? Every sale is the same. And they're like, what? I'm like, every sale is the same. Watch. It's a goddamn straight line. And I grabbed my trusty marker and I said, you have it open and you have a close. And I drew the straight line on the boy said, this is your open and this is your close. And your goals of salesperson is to keep them right on the straight line and pushing them all the way from the open to the close.
